justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Three distinct integration patterns clearly identified — SAP as batch/SFTP with 15-min polling, DHL as webhook/REST with async acknowledgment, Analytics as Kafka stream consumer. (2) DHL 500ms constraint addressed with dedicated Webhook Gateway that immediately returns 200 OK and enqueues to 'dhl.inbound.raw' topic for async processing. (3) DHL webhook idempotency explicitly addressed — deduplicated by trackingId+status, called out in patterns_used and pitfalls_to_avoid. (4) SAP silent failure risk identified as high severity — polling gap where files deposited and removed between polls are lost, with reconciliation job mitigation. (5) Dedicated integration layer via separate ACL adapters per external system, explicitly recommending against embedding logic in domain services. (6) Flexibility points clearly state new carriers (FedEx, UPS) require only a new adapter with no changes to core domain.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — goes well beyond minimum pass criteria with transactional outbox pattern, circuit breakers, dead letter queues, and phased implementation guidance
Correctly identifies the Conformist relationship with SAP (cannot influence their XML schema) vs Customer-Supplier for internal contexts
Includes a DHL polling fallback as safety net for unreliable webhook delivery — a risk not in the rubric but practically important
Rigidity points section honestly acknowledges Kafka lock-in and event schema as intentional constraints
Pitfalls_to_avoid section directly addresses every failure mode in the rubric — no business logic in adapters, no synchronous webhook processing, mandatory idempotency
Could more explicitly name the three patterns as 'batch', 'event-driven/webhook', and 'stream' rather than requiring the reader to infer the taxonomy from the component descriptions
